_meta.what_firm_does: OpenAI is the AI research and deployment company behind ChatGPT and the GPT models. Its stated mission is to ensure that artificial general intelligence benefits all of humanity.
_meta.jd_wants: A detail-obsessed People Ops specialist who keeps the employee-lifecycle running cleanly — data integrity, process design, cross-functional support, and automation that scales with rapid growth.
_meta.status: DRAFT — Jamie to review/edit before submit

Why are you interested in this role?: People Operations isn't just about keeping systems updated—from my experience, it's about building the data integrity and process infrastructure that lets every lifecycle moment run cleanly. This role is that instinct made into a job. At InGenius Prep my core skill is deconstructing a process to find the root cause and redesigning the workflow around it—work that cut onboarding time by 75% for 70+ staff—and I increasingly lean on AI tools to automate the repetitive parts and improve accuracy. I'm the person who notices the friction in a process and quietly engineers it out, then documents it so it stays fixed. Doing that for a team scaling as fast as OpenAI's is exactly the kind of problem I want.
Why OpenAI?: Two honest reasons. First, the mission—ensuring AI benefits all of humanity—is work I'd be glad to keep the operational engine running behind. Second, this is genuinely what I do well: I treat People operations as a system to diagnose and improve, not just transactions to process, and I already use AI tools daily in my own work, so a People Ops team that builds automations to scale with growth feels like home rather than a stretch. I'd rather be the careful person behind reliable systems at a place doing work this consequential than polish processes somewhere the stakes are lower.
What excites you about this opportunity?: What excites me is the rare combination of high stakes and clean systems. Most operations work is either careful but slow or fast but messy; a People Ops function at OpenAI has to be both—detail-unforgiving on data and fast enough to keep up with the company. At Vestas I directed an M&A project migrating and merging employee records with zero tolerance for error, and I learned how much downstream trust depends on getting the data right the first time. The chance to build that kind of dependable infrastructure while the team is scaling, and to use automation to make it scale better rather than just adding headcount, is the part I'd genuinely look forward to.
Tell us about a time you improved a brok: At InGenius Prep, onboarding 70+ cross-functional staff across Marketing, Tech, and Sales was slow and inconsistent—every new hire's experience depended on who happened to help them. Rather than patch it case by case, I mapped where it actually broke down, then built standardized documentation, SOPs, and reusable workflows, automating the repetitive administrative steps. That cut onboarding time by 75% and, more importantly, made the experience consistent regardless of who was around. It taught me that durable fixes start with diagnosing the underlying system, not the symptom—which is exactly how my MS in Applied Organizational Psychology trained me to think about people problems: diagnose with data, collaborate cross-functionally on the fix, then track whether it held.
